name: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid
title: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA): The First to Believe
meta_description: The story of Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA), the first person to accept Islam and the wife who held the Prophet Muhammad after the first revelation. Her character, generosity, and steadfast support.
keywords: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, story of Khadijah, first Muslim, wife of Prophet Muhammad, mothers of the believers, sahaba stories
lede: Before there was a single mosque, before a single verse was recited in public, before anyone on earth had said the word "Muslim," there was a woman in Makkah who looked at a frightened man and told him, calmly, that God would never abandon him.

This is the story of Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (may Allah be pleased with her): the most honoured woman of Makkah, the first person to accept Islam, and the steadfast support behind the first revelation.

sections.h2: The most honoured woman in Makkah
paras: Khadijah was born around the year 555, into one of the noble houses of Quraysh. She shared the same lineage as the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ back to their common ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab. Her father, Khuwaylid, was a chief who had once stood ready to defend the Kaaba.

Long before Islam, people called her Tahirah: the pure one. It was said of her that she never lied, never backbit, and never hurt or upset anyone. She was beautiful, literate, and sharp, and she never bowed to the idols of her people.

She was also, by any measure of her time, immensely successful. Widowed twice by the age of 25, she grew her wealth into the largest trading caravan in Makkah. Over her roof flew a green silk pavilion, and it was not a banner of wealth: it was a sign to the poor that this was a house where they would be helped.

Tired of being cheated by merchants, Khadijah looked for an honest man to take her goods to Syria. Her sister pointed her to a young shepherd known across Makkah for one thing above all: he was al-Amin, the trustworthy. She hired him, and her servant Maysarah returned with story after story of his honesty on the road.

When a friend suggested marriage, the Prophet ﷺ wondered why a woman of her standing would ever accept him. She had already turned down some of the most powerful men in Makkah. Yet when she told him why she wanted him, she did not mention his lineage or his looks. She said: "I have come to you because of your noble character and your truthful tongue."
She was about 40, he was about 25. For the next 25 years he married no one else. Years later, the Qur'an would describe the One who had quietly arranged all of this:

ayah.text: Did He not find you an orphan and shelter you? Did He not find you lost and guide you? Did He not find you in need and make you self-sufficient?

sections.h2: The cave, and the words that held him
paras: By his late thirties, the Prophet ﷺ had everything a man of Makkah could want. Then the dreams began, and a love of solitude drew him up to a cave on the mountain of Hira. The climb takes the better part of an hour. Khadijah, by then in her fifties, made that climb herself to carry him food and water so he would not have to break his retreat.

It was there that the angel Jibril came to him. He returned trembling and broke into the house saying, "Cover me, cover me." She held him until the fear left him. When he was finally able to speak, he confided the thing he feared most: that he had lost his mind.

In that moment, a single wrong word could have broken him. She chose hers perfectly. "No," she said. "Never. Rejoice. By Allah, Allah would never disgrace you." Then she told him why: he kept good relations, spoke only the truth, carried the burdens of others, and stood by those in hardship. She believed in him before he was certain of himself. Soon the command came:

ayah.text: You, wrapped in your cloak, arise and give warning! Proclaim the greatness of your Lord; cleanse yourself; keep away from all filth.

sections.h2: Standing with him through the boycott
paras: "You are the first I am calling to this," he said. "And I am the first to accept it," she answered. With that, Khadijah became the first believer in the history of Islam.

As the message spread, so did the persecution, and she spent her great fortune on the cause until little of it was left. When Quraysh boycotted the Prophet's clan, Khadijah did not have to enter the valley of hardship. She belonged to a different clan and could have stayed in the comfort of her home. She chose the valley.

A woman who had lived her whole life in elegance was reduced to eating what she could find to survive, and she never once complained. In those years the angel Jibril came with a message meant only for her: that Allah Himself sent her peace, and the glad tidings of a home in Paradise made of pearl, where there would be no noise and no weariness.

Khadijah died around the year 620, in what became known as the Year of Sorrow. The Prophet ﷺ buried her himself, lowering into the grave the woman whose arms had held him when he came down from the mountain. Those close to him feared his grief might take him too.

He never forgot her. Years later, our mother Aisha (RA) narrated that not a day passed without him mentioning Khadijah. When he conquered Makkah at last, returning in triumph to the city that had driven him out, he did not stay in its finest homes. He pitched his tent near her grave.

When Aisha once asked whether Allah had not given him someone better, his answer was firm: "She believed in me when no one else did. She supported me with her wealth when others held back." And in another narration, a line of pure tenderness: "I was blessed with her love."

Her place in this series is earned many times over. She was the first to believe. She was the first to pray beside the Prophet ﷺ, the first to make wudu and the first to make tawaf at his side. She was the first person in this ummah to receive salaam from Allah, carried to her by the angel Jibril.

She did not live to see the religion she believed in take its final shape. She never prayed the five daily prayers as we know them, never fasted a Ramadan of obligation, never saw Madinah. Her whole share of Islam was its hardest, earliest years, and she gave herself to them completely.
When the people of Makkah counted their great names, hers was not on the list. When Allah counted the first of the believers, hers was the first name of all.

lessons_intro: Her story is not a single dramatic moment. It is a quarter of a century of steadiness, and that is exactly why it endures.

lessons.text: She chose the Prophet ﷺ for his honesty and his good character, the same qualities the Qur'an would later define as righteousness.
She did not preach in the streets. She held a frightened man, said the right words, and gave him the ground to stand on.

lessons.bold: Real generosity asks for nothing back.
lessons.text: She spent her wealth, her standing, and her comfort, and considered the gratitude of Allah enough.
She walked into the boycott by choice and never complained her way back out of it.

faq.a: A respected and successful merchant of Makkah and the first wife of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She was the first person to accept Islam and is honoured as the first of the Mothers of the Believers.

faq.q: Why is Khadijah called the first believer?
faq.a: When the Prophet ﷺ returned from the cave of Hira after the first revelation and told her what had happened, she accepted his message without hesitation, before any other man, woman, or child.

faq.q: How old was Khadijah when she married the Prophet?
faq.a: According to the well-known narrations, she was about 40 and the Prophet about 25. They were married for about 25 years, until her death, and he did not marry anyone else in her lifetime.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Khadijah?
faq.a: The weight of good character, the strength of quiet and steadfast support, and generosity that asks for nothing in return.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to imagine that faith needs a stage: a battle, a migration, a public stand. Khadijah's life says otherwise. Her faith showed itself in a steady voice at the worst possible moment, in wealth quietly spent, in a climb up a mountain with food for a husband who needed her. None of it was loud. All of it mattered.

Most of us are tested the way she was: not in front of crowds, but in our own homes, with the people who depend on us. Her life is an invitation to be the one who steadies others, who gives without keeping count, who believes in what is right before it becomes easy. Carry one of those into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Khadijah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Qur'an translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 93:6-8 and 74:1-5). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
Before a single mosque.

Before a single verse was recited in public.

Before anyone on earth had said the word "Muslim."

There was Khadijah.

Makkah called her **Tahirah**, the pure one, long before Islam arrived.

She never lied. She never backbit. She never bowed to an idol.

She ran the largest trading caravan in the city.

Over her roof flew a green pavilion: a quiet sign to the poor that here, they would be helped.

She married him for one reason.

Not his wealth. Not his lineage.

"I have come to you because of your noble character and your truthful tongue."

He came down from the cave of Hira shaking. "Cover me," he said.

She held him. Then she said the words that held him: "By Allah, Allah would never disgrace you."

"You are the first I am calling to this."

"And I am the first to accept it."

The first believer in the history of Islam was Khadijah (RA).

When Makkah starved his clan in the boycott, she could have stayed in the comfort of her home.

She chose the valley. She gave away her fortune. She never once complained.

**Quiet support can change history.**

She did not preach in the streets. She gave one frightened man the ground to stand on.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
The first person to believe in Islam was not a warrior or a chief. It was Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (may Allah be pleased with her): a successful merchant, known across Makkah as "the pure" long before the revelation came.

When the Prophet ﷺ came down from the cave of Hira afraid he had lost his mind, she did not question him. She held him, and she reminded him of who he was. She was the first to believe, and she spent everything she had on a cause she never lived to see flourish.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/khadijah

email.subject: The first person to believe was a woman named Khadijah
email.alt_subject: "By Allah, Allah would never disgrace you"
email.preview: She held the Prophet when he came down from the cave, and said the words that held him.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The first person to accept Islam was not a chief or a warrior. It was a woman: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (may Allah be pleased with her).

Long before the revelation, Makkah already had a name for her. They called her Tahirah, the pure one. She ran the largest trading caravan in the city, and over her roof flew a green pavilion, a quiet sign to the poor that this was a house where they would be helped.

When she married the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, she told him plainly why. Not his lineage. Not his wealth. "I have come to you," she said, "because of your noble character and your truthful tongue."

Years later, he came down from the cave of Hira trembling. She held him until the fear passed. And when he confided that he was afraid he had lost his mind, she did not hesitate. "No," she said. "Never. Allah would never disgrace you."

She believed in him before he was certain of himself. "You are the first I am calling to this," he said. "And I am the first to accept it," she answered.

She would spend her entire fortune on the cause, and walk willingly into the years of the boycott when she could have stayed home in comfort. She never once complained.

There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss: quiet support can change history. She did not preach in the streets. She gave one frightened man the ground to stand on.

May Allah be pleased with her, and gather us in the company of the first to believe.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Before there was a single mosque, before a single verse was recited in public, before anyone on earth had said the word "Muslim," there was a woman in Makkah who looked at a frightened man and told him, calmly, that God would never abandon him.

Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (may Allah be pleased with her) was already remarkable before Islam. Her people called her Tahirah, the pure one. Widowed twice by the age of twenty-five, she grew her inheritance into the largest trading caravan in Makkah, and over her roof flew a green pavilion: a quiet sign to the poor that this was a house where they would be helped.

When she sought an honest man to carry her goods, she was pointed to a young shepherd known across the city as al-Amin, the trustworthy. She would marry him, and when she explained why, she named neither his wealth nor his lineage. "I have come to you," she said, "because of your noble character and your truthful tongue."

Then came the cave. The Prophet returned from Hira trembling, and burst into their home saying, "Cover me, cover me." She held him until the fear left him. When he confided that he feared he had lost his mind, she did not waver. "No. Never. Rejoice," she said, and reminded him of every good thing he was. "You are the first I am calling to this," he told her. "And I am the first to accept it," she answered, and so the first believer in the history of Islam was Khadijah.

She gave the rest to the cause. When Quraysh boycotted his clan, she left the comfort of her own home to enter the valley of hardship, and there a woman who had lived her whole life in elegance ate what she could find to survive, and never once complained. In those years the angel Jibril brought her a greeting of peace from Allah Himself, and the glad tidings of a home in Paradise made of pearl.

She died in the Year of Sorrow, and the Prophet buried her with his own hands. He never forgot her. When he returned to Makkah in triumph at the end of his life, he did not stay in its finest homes. He pitched his tent near her grave.
storybook.pullquote: By Allah, Allah would never disgrace you.
storybook.reflection: Khadijah's story is not one dramatic moment but a quarter-century of steadiness. She did not preach in the streets; she gave one frightened man the ground to stand on. Quiet, faithful support, the kind that asks for nothing in return, can carry a message further than the loudest voice.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
